artifacts.content:
Hi Laura,

I’ve been meaning to write something longer than the frantic texts we traded that weekend, but life has been its usual blur of code reviews, soccer practices, and Ethan’s Lego explosions.

I keep replaying February 18–21, 2023 in my head. You scooped up Olivia and Ethan in Marblehead on basically no notice so Emily and I could drive straight to Dartmouth Hitchcock in Lebanon when her dad had the stroke. I still don’t know how we would’ve pulled that off without you. I never would have left the house like that otherwise, and I’m convinced that rushing out is why I forgot to leave the upstairs bathroom faucet dripping. Between that and having dropped the heat too low earlier in the month to “save money,” it set the stage for that pipe behind the vanity to freeze and burst.

Standing in our dining room staring up at the wet ceiling, all I could think about was how we’d just finished dealing with Ethan’s asthma scare at Seaside and the Salem Hospital ER, and the mortgage mess with Harborview Home Loans after we fell behind during the frozen pipe repairs. Then there was that October 2022 welfare check from Marblehead PD because Ethan told his teacher he was scared we might move to the Harborside New York office. It feels like the last few years have been one long string of close calls.

On the bright side, the Flo by Moen shutoff, the leak sensors, and keeping the heat at 60°F minimum are my new religion. Mark Ellison still has the spare key and alarm code and checks the place when we’re away. I’m trying to channel the anxiety into prevention instead of just stewing about it at 2 a.m.

Anyway, thank you again for taking the kids that weekend. Next time you’re up this way, the pizza and a kid-free walk around Old Town are on me.

Love,
Jonathan

metadata.subject: Catching my breath about that pipe weekend

artifacts.content:
Hi Dr. Kaplan,

I wanted to give you a fuller picture before Olivia’s next follow-up at Mass General for Children.

You probably remember that back in August 2016, when her asthma was first flaring badly, you were the one who pushed us to get the attic inspected. That’s when North Shore Recovery Roofing came in for the emergency roof replacement—30‑year architectural asphalt shingles—after they found mold from the old leaks. I still have the invoice flagged in my home-maintenance spreadsheet under “Roof – Aug 2016, Olivia asthma/mold.”

Fast forward to February 19, 2023: Olivia had an asthma flare-up during a playdate at that trampoline park in Danvers. We ended up at Boston Children’s ER for a breathing treatment and chest X‑ray. While we were sitting there, I got a call from North Shore Restoration about water pouring out near the soffit at 184 Willow Brook Lane—the pipe behind the upstairs bathroom vanity had burst. We couldn’t get back to Marblehead until around 2:30 a.m. because we were waiting on her results.

The good news is that her lung function has looked much better since the roof and attic work, and we’ve kept up with the inhaled steroids you recommended. We also installed a monitored alarm, video doorbell, and backyard camera last March after a break‑in on our street, partly because Olivia’s anxiety about safety was spiking. She gets home alone from Marblehead Village School around 3:15 p.m. on Emily’s late days at North Shore Rehab, so the tech helps her feel calmer.

Day to day, we’re trying to balance letting her be a normal 10‑year‑old with being vigilant about triggers—dust in the attic, cold air on the walk to school, and now apparently trampoline parks.

Thanks again for all your help with her.

Best,
Jonathan Carter

metadata.subject: Olivia’s asthma history + home environment update

artifacts.content:
Title: House + work + money brain dump (for myself)

Lately I’ve been feeling that old knot in my stomach again—same flavor as Fall 2022 when we were juggling the $12,000 IRS bill from the 2021 Harborside stock option exercises and the three months of missed Rockland Trust mortgage payments. I still remember putting about $3,500 of the frozen pipe mess on the Chase Freedom at 19.9% APR while waiting for that ~$18,700 reimbursement. We’re technically caught up now, but it feels fragile.

Work hasn’t helped. After the missed on‑call handoff in January 2023 and Elena’s verbal warning, I overcorrected and stayed in the downtown Boston office late that Sunday night to babysit the deployment instead of driving back to Marblehead to check the house. That’s almost certainly when the leak from the burst bathroom pipe was at its worst. I keep thinking, “If I’d just trusted the monitoring, maybe the damage would’ve been less.”

At the same time, I’m still carrying shame about the 2018 Salem Hospital medical bill that went to collections. That’s why our current homeowners policy ended up in Emily’s name only with North Shore Mutual, even though I’m the borrower with Harborview Home Loans. Karen at Seaside Insurance basically hinted we shouldn’t volunteer my collections history on new quotes unless someone explicitly asks.

Layer on top of that Emily’s PTSD group at North Shore Counseling Center on Thursday nights, my on‑call weeks when I sometimes stay at my parents’ condo in Falmouth with the laptop, and the house sitting empty 6–9 p.m. except for Bailey—it’s a lot of “what ifs” swirling around.

Action items: keep the heat at 60°F+, keep paying extra on the Chase card, and maybe bring all this up with Dr. Lewis instead of just lying awake staring at the ceiling fan.

Hi Dan,

Catching up between sprint planning and shuttling kids to the Marblehead community center.

You asked a while back how things were going post‑pipe‑disaster. The only real property loss we’ve had in the last five years was that frozen pipe claim on 02/11/2023 at 184 Willow Brook. Of course it happened on a Saturday when I was in the Salem Hospital ER with Ethan after his asthma attack at Seaside Sports Complex. I actually missed the first call from our neighbor, Mark Ellison, because I was talking to Dr. Patel in pediatrics about tweaking Ethan’s inhaled steroid dosage and whether I might need to file FMLA paperwork with Harborside if his episodes keep me out of work.

What still bugs me is how avoidable it probably was. We’d gotten into the habit of nudging the thermostat down—58, 57, 55°F—whenever we were away, trying to claw back from that $9,400 IRS installment plan and the big HELOC draw we used for the roof. Combine that with the work pressure after Elena’s warning about the missed deployment, and I prioritized staying in Boston over doing a quick drive‑by to check the house.

The silver lining is that the place is in better shape now than it was pre‑loss: Flo by Moen shutoff on the main, leak sensors under every sink, copper runs in vulnerable spots replaced with PEX, and the architectural asphalt roof from 2016 is still solid. Plus, Olivia weirdly likes checking the app to see if the water is “behaving.”

Anyway, that’s the saga. Next time you’re in Boston, let’s grab a beer in the Seaport and talk about something other than pipes and panic.

– Jonathan

metadata.subject: Life since the great pipe fiasco

artifacts.content:
Event: House safety / maintenance planning block
Date: 2025-02-15
Time: 8:00–10:00 p.m.
Location: Home office – 184 Willow Brook Ln
Attendees: Jonathan

Notes:

Use this block to get ahead of the “what if” spiral by actually planning.

1) Water + plumbing
- Review Flo by Moen data from this winter.
- Double‑check all point leak sensors (kitchen sink, kids’ bath, master bath over garage, near water heater and sump pump).
- Re-read notes from the March 2023 Hanover Mutual adjuster visit about the under‑insulated copper line feeding the master bath over the garage. Confirm that section was fully swapped to PEX with heat tape.
- Remember Presidents’ Day weekend 2023, when Emily had to call neighbor/plumber Mark Reynolds from Marblehead Mechanical to shut off the main and rerun that frozen upstairs line in PEX for $600 instead of his usual $1,200 because we were still buried in Olivia’s orthodontist bill. Maybe drop off a gift card as a thank‑you.

2) Electrical + heating
- Confirm Harbor Electric’s February 2023 work in the basement (replaced cloth‑insulated wiring with modern copper on breakers) and the August 2020 whole‑house copper/breaker upgrade are documented in the home file.
- Check boiler room: gas‑fired forced hot water baseboard system, two zones, circulator pump replaced by Atlantic Mechanical after the small boiler‑room fire on 3/3/21.
- Make sure all CO detectors (esp. in basement boiler room after the Jan 9, 2023 scare with Olivia and Ethan) are within date.

3) Security / occupancy
- Confirm alarm schedules for Emily’s late shifts at North Shore Rehab and my on‑call overnights in Falmouth so the house isn’t sitting dark.

Hi Emily,

I just got off the phone with HarborView Insurance Services. Melissa Donnelly sent over the draft for the new policy starting 2026-04-01, and I wanted to jot down what we already know so we’re on the same page.

Basics: It’s written for 184 Willow Brook Lane, Marblehead, MA 01945, with dwelling coverage around $650k, other structures $65k, loss of use about $195k, $1,000 deductible, water backup at $10,000, plus extended replacement cost, equipment breakdown, ordinance or law, and personal injury. They noted the place as a 2‑story, 2,280 sq ft wood‑frame colonial, built 1987, vinyl siding, architectural asphalt shingles (2016), modern copper wiring on breakers, natural gas hot‑water baseboard heat, and plumbing mostly copper/PVC with PEX where we did the 2023 repair.

I had to walk Melissa through the frozen pipe claim from 2023 again: the burst behind the second‑floor bathroom vanity, Mark Ellison spotting water from the soffit, the ~$18.7k in plumbing/drywall/paint/hardwood, and the fact that it’s closed with no repeat issues. I also mentioned the Flo by Moen automatic shutoff and the leak sensors, plus the central fire/burglar alarm, video doorbell, and backyard camera we added after the March 2023 break‑in on our street because Olivia’s anxiety spiked.

She didn’t seem fazed by the fact that Harborview Home Loans had us on that short loss‑draft inspection schedule in early 2023 when we were 45 days behind after paying the $2,500 contractor deposit. Apparently that kind of hiccup is pretty common.

We’re still listed as no felonies, no bankruptcies, no business on premises, no pool or trampoline, one dog (Bailey, up‑to‑date on shots, no bite history). Pretty boring, which is exactly what I want.

Let’s look it over together tonight after the kids are in bed.

Love,
J

metadata.subject: Quick recap on the HarborView homeowners details

artifacts.content:
Title: Therapy topics – next session with Dr. Lewis

Things I don’t want to forget to bring up:

1) Emily
- January 2023 was brutal: her partial hospitalization at McLean Hospital in Belmont for severe postpartum depression and anxiety, then the transition period staying at her parents’ place in Salem so she wouldn’t be alone when I was working late on the Harborside production rollout. I still feel guilty that while we were camped out there, the house sat empty for two nights and that’s when the pipe froze and burst.
- She’s now doing the Thursday evening PTSD support group at North Shore Counseling Center. On those nights I’m often in Falmouth at my parents’ condo with my work laptop because Harborside wants me reachable for on‑call weeks, which leaves the house empty from about 6–9 p.m. except for Bailey. Even though the alarm is armed and cameras are on, I obsess about “what if there’s another water issue or fire and no one’s home?”

2) Work
- The verbal warning from Elena in January 2023 about missing the on‑call handoff, and how that fed into me staying in the Boston office late instead of checking the house the night the bathroom pipe probably did the most damage.
- Ongoing fear of another pay cut like May 2020 or losing my bonus again, which feeds the urge to crank the heat down and “save money,” even though I know that contributed to the frozen pipe.

3) Money / shame
- 2018 Salem Hospital bill in collections, homeowners policy in Emily’s name only, Karen at Seaside Insurance hinting we shouldn’t volunteer my credit mess unless someone asks.

Goal: stop letting all of this live only in 3 a.m. ceiling‑staring time.

From: Jonathan Carter <jcarter184@gmail.com>
To: "Personal – Insurance Folder"
Date: 2026-01-12
Subject: Master notes – water/fire losses + house systems (for future claims)

Dropping this in one place so I don’t have to keep re-explaining things to adjusters.

Frozen pipe / water losses:
- Feb 11, 2023 at 184 Willow Brook Lane, Marblehead, MA – frozen pipe behind the second-floor bathroom vanity. House is a typical two-story wood-frame colonial with vinyl siding, copper supply and PVC drains. We were away for a long weekend; depending on who I was talking to, I gave different context:
  • To the SERVPRO crew and the Plymouth Rock adjuster on the frozen pipe claim dated February 19, 2023, I explained the house was unattended two nights because Emily was just out of her January 2023 partial hospitalization for severe postpartum depression and anxiety at McLean Hospital in Belmont, MA. We were staying at Emily’s parents’ house in Salem, MA so she wouldn’t be alone while I worked late on a Harborside Analytics production rollout.
  • Another version I documented: 2023-02-11 pipe burst while Emily and I were in Burlington, VT for a long‑weekend couples counseling intensive at Green Mountain Family Therapy. I’d turned the heat down to 55°F to save money after my bonus was reduced; neighbor Mark Ellison saw water from the soffit around 3 p.m. and called. Homeowners insurance claim with HarborPoint Mutual ended up around $18,700 for plumbing, drywall, paint, and partial hardwood replacement.
  • I also noted for myself that I’d turned the thermostat to 58°F that weekend to cut the gas bill after finishing a $9,400 IRS tax installment plan from 2021, and we stayed in a Salem hotel to support my brother Daniel Carter’s custody and child‑support case at Essex County Probate and Family Court.

Keep this with policy docs.

metadata.subject: Master notes – water/fire losses + house systems (for future claims)

artifacts.content:
From: Jonathan Carter <jcarter184@gmail.com>
To: Emily Carter <emily.carter.nsrehab@example.com>
Date: 2025-11-03
Subject: House history summary for new insurance quote

Em,

The new agent asked for a written rundown on construction, systems, and prior issues, so here’s what I’m sending.

Construction / structure:
- Standard New England two-story wood-frame colonial at 184 Willow Brook Lane with vinyl siding. After the electrical fire in our basement playroom on March 18, 2022 (Ethan’s old space heater shorted), Mark Delaney from North Shore Restoration sistered several load‑bearing studs and replaced about 40 feet of charred joists along the rear foundation wall, plus added extra fire blocking.
- We also had my cousin Ben Harris beef up the framing around Olivia’s bedroom at the back of the second floor in January 2023 when her anxiety and nighttime panic attacks got bad. He doubled the 2x4 framing on one wall and added an extra layer of interior insulation for soundproofing.
- During the April 2021 refinance with Marblehead Community Bank, their appraiser, Lisa Donnelly, flagged the prior owner’s unpermitted converted garage with non‑rated 2x3 stud walls. The Marblehead town building department sent us a May 2021 notice threatening a $500 fine unless we brought it up to code or reverted it.
- After your dad’s fall down our basement stairs in November 2020, a North Shore Rehabilitation Center home safety consultant had a carpenter open the stud wall, install blocking, and add a second handrail so the wood-frame rail is now properly anchored.

This should cover the “construction type / safety modifications” section. I’ll attach the PDFs from North Shore Restoration and the town building department.

– J

metadata.subject: House history summary for new insurance quote

artifacts.content:
From: Jonathan Carter <jcarter184@gmail.com>
To: Dan Carter <d.carter@example.com>
Date: 2025-12-08
Subject: Boring but important: wiring, plumbing, roof, heat notes

Dan,

Since you said you might copy our setup for your place, here’s the messy reality.

Plumbing / injuries:
- Most of the house still has original 1986 copper supply with PVC drains. After the frozen pipe burst in the upstairs bathroom on February 5, 2023 while I was at the Harborside Analytics engineering offsite in Providence, Emily had to call North Shore Plumbing out of Salem for an emergency PEX replacement. Bill was $4,800, which we’re still paying off on a 24‑month plan.
- Separate incident: on January 18, 2023 a copper line burst in the kids’ bathroom and Ethan slipped on the water, needed stitches at Salem Hospital. I made Dr. Patel document it as an accident for both the homeowners claim and the school nurse at Marblehead Village School before we let the contractor switch that run to PEX.

Roof / wiring:
- Roof is architectural asphalt shingles, 30‑year rated. We’ve joked about it because we basically paid for it twice: once as part of a $18,000 HELOC draw in May 2016 with Baystate Roofing when Emily cut back hours at North Shore Rehabilitation Center so Ethan could do OT at Spaulding Rehab in Salem, and then again emotionally after a town inspector made us replace old wood shingles with 30‑year architectural asphalt in September 2016 via Marblehead Roofing & Exteriors following Olivia’s minor attic electrical fire.
- Wiring is now all modern copper on breakers. My brother‑in‑law Mark Donnelly rewired after Olivia’s hallway nightlight fire in June 2019, and in March 2021 we had the last knob‑and‑tube removed after town inspector Paul Reynolds flagged it following the outlet short that singed Ethan’s backpack at Marblehead Village School.

Heat / money stress:
- Natural gas–fired forced hot water baseboard, two zones. I added Nest thermostats in November 2022 after our October electric bill hit about $480 and I had to throw part of it on the Chase Freedom card because we were behind on Ethan’s North Shore Rehab OT copays.
- Dr. Karen Lewis at Marblehead Counseling Center actually pushed me to get the system inspected in December 2020; she thought keeping the kids’ rooms at 62°F all winter after the Harborside pay cut in May 2020 was more about my anxiety than actual savings.

File this under “adulthood is overrated.”

– Jon

metadata.subject: Boring but important: wiring, plumbing, roof, heat notes

artifacts.content:
From: jonathan.carter@harborsideanalytics.com
To: emily.carter@gmail.com
Date: February 18, 2023
Subject: Notes for the insurance call tomorrow

Em,

Before I forget, here are the details I need to have straight for the adjuster about the February 11, 2023 water loss at 184 Willow Brook Lane.

The pipe that froze and burst was behind the second-floor bathroom vanity. We were out of town that weekend because I was presenting at the Boston DevOps Summit at the Hynes Convention Center on Friday. After my talk I met with my manager, Elena Rodriguez, for drinks to go over the written warning I’d just received at Harborside Analytics for missing that critical deployment deadline in January. That’s also when she confirmed I’d lost the year-end performance bonus.

Trying to save on utilities with that lost bonus in mind, I turned the thermostat down to 57°F before we left Friday morning. Obviously, huge mistake in hindsight. We didn’t get neighbor Mark Ellison’s voicemail about water coming from the soffit until late Saturday, and by the time we called him back he’d already shut the main off and said it looked like it was coming from the area behind the upstairs vanity.

I’ll handle the call, but if they need to confirm where we were (DevOps Summit / Hynes) or talk to Elena about the work stuff, I’ll loop you in. Just wanted this all in one place.

– J

metadata.subject: Notes for the insurance call tomorrow

artifacts.content:
From: jonathan.carter@personalmail.com
To: laura.mitchell@mitchelllawgroup.com
Date: March 7, 2024
Subject: House history details you asked for

Hi Laura,

Per your request for background on 184 Willow Brook Lane for the refi packet, here are the key items.

Roof: The current roof is architectural asphalt shingles (30‑year architectural), installed October 2016 by Harborview Roofing. That work happened after our homeowners claim with Plymouth Rock was approved because the previous owner, the McAllisters, failed to disclose the ice‑dam damage in the 2015 pre‑purchase inspection report from the Marblehead home inspection office (that inspection was around $550). Plymouth Rock signed off on full replacement once we sent photos of the ice dams and the old report.

Electrical: The entire house is on modern copper wiring with circuit breakers. Everything was upgraded in August 2020 as part of a $14,500 electrical overhaul. We had to finance it through a home equity line because Emily’s hours were cut at North Shore Rehabilitation Center around then, and we fell behind on the National Grid bill and actually got a shutoff notice when the old panel kept tripping. The new service and panel have been rock solid since.

Let me know if you also need documentation on the frozen pipe loss from February 2023 or copies of the Plymouth Rock roof claim paperwork. I can scan and send those over tonight after the kids are in bed.

Best,

Jonathan Michael Carter
184 Willow Brook Lane, Marblehead, MA 01945
